attributes.stem: An 82-year-old woman is brought to the General Practice clinic by her daughter. The daughter reports that her mother has been having disturbed sleep and seems to be having difficulty using her hands. The patient has moderate dementia and is unable to provide a clear history. She has no significant medical history other than hypertension and takes amlodipine. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She is unable to follow complex commands. When asked to demonstrate hand movements, she appears clumsy and struggles to manipulate small objects. The following is observed on inspection of her hands (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following nerves is most likely to be affected based on the physical sign shown?
attributes.explanation:
The image shows significant wasting of the thenar eminence, which is a characteristic late sign of carpal tunnel syndrome. This finding suggests long-standing compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist. The median nerve (C5-T1) innervates the muscles of the thenar eminence, including the opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, and the superficial head of flexor pollicis brevis. Prolonged compression leads to atrophy of these muscles, resulting in the visible wasting.

The ulnar nerve (C8-T1) innervates the hypothenar muscles and interossei. Damage to this nerve would typically result in wasting of the hypothenar eminence and the interosseous muscles, causing hollowing between the metacarpals, which is not evident in this image.

The radial nerve (C5-T1) primarily innervates the extensor muscles of the forearm and hand. Damage to this nerve would result in wrist drop and inability to extend the fingers, which is not consistent with the image provided.

The lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm and the medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm are purely sensory nerves. Damage to these nerves would not result in muscle wasting or motor deficits as seen in the image.

In patients with communication difficulties, such as those with dementia, careful observation and physical examination become even more crucial. The daughter's report of disturbed sleep and difficulty using hands are consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome, where patients often experience nighttime paresthesias and clumsiness with fine motor tasks. However, the diagnosis in this case is primarily based on the characteristic physical finding of thenar wasting.

It's important to note that while thenar wasting is a specific sign of median nerve compression, it is a late finding. Earlier symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome can include pain, numbness, and tingling in the distribution of the median nerve (thumb, index, middle finger, and radial half of the ring finger). Management would typically involve wrist splinting, consideration of corticosteroid injections, and possibly surgical decompression, taking into account the patient's overall health status and ability to participate in treatment.
learningPoints: Wasting of the thenar eminence is a late sign of carpal tunnel syndrome.
Clinical examination and observation are crucial in patients with communication difficulties.
references: Robertson, S., Smith, D. Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm. In: Radiopaedia.org [online]. 2018; cited 2022 Jun 27. Available at: https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-58138
Thomas K, Sajjad H, Bordoni B. Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Medial Brachial Cutaneous Nerve. In: StatPearls [online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539829/
Patel M, Varacallo M. Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Arm Nerves. In: StatPearls [online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547735/
